nodes.title: Advanced Functional and Data-Driven Materials Map
nodes.summary: A map for complex alloys, nanostructured systems, functional response, oxidation, and computational materials design.

nodes.title: Biomaterials and Surface Response Map
nodes.summary: A map connecting biomaterials, biocompatibility, wear, corrosion, surface modification, and the coupled mechanical-chemical response of implanted or bio-exposed systems.

nodes.title: Characterization and Failure Analysis Map
nodes.summary: A map that connects evidence-gathering methods to defect analysis, corrosion, fracture, and service diagnosis.

nodes.title: Characterization Methods Overview
nodes.summary: A top-level map of characterization and measurement methods across structure, composition, properties, thermal response, electrochemistry, and failure analysis.

nodes.title: Defects Interfaces and Microstructure Map
nodes.summary: A map for the structural features that control real-material behavior by disrupting ideal order and organizing matter at intermediate scales.

nodes.title: Electrochemical and Soft Materials Map
nodes.summary: A map connecting electrochemical interfaces, polymer response, soft processing, and application clusters such as batteries and biomaterials.

nodes.title: Electronic Magnetic and Optical Properties Map
nodes.summary: A map for charge transport, polarization, magnetization, and light-matter interaction in functional materials.

nodes.title: Energy Electronic and Biomedical Materials Map
nodes.summary: A map for application clusters where performance targets sharply constrain structure, chemistry, and processing.

nodes.title: Environmental and Chemical Degradation Map
nodes.summary: A map for failure modes driven by corrosion, oxidation, hydrogen uptake, stress-assisted chemical attack, and surface-film breakdown.

nodes.title: Fracture and Crack Propagation Map
nodes.summary: A map for crack initiation, stable and unstable crack growth, and the major fracture modes that control structural failure.

nodes.title: Materials Failure Overview
nodes.summary: A top-level map of materials failure modes across fracture, fatigue, creep, corrosion, oxidation, wear, and coupled environment-assisted damage.

nodes.title: Materials Properties Overview
nodes.summary: A top-level map of mechanical, thermal, electrical, magnetic, dielectric, optical, and transport-related properties used to evaluate material performance.

nodes.title: Materials Science Overview
nodes.summary: A top-level map of the structure-processing-properties-performance view of materials science and engineering.

nodes.title: Mechanical and Environmental Testing Map
nodes.summary: A map for methods that measure response under load, impact, indentation, corrosion, electrochemical bias, or thermal program.

nodes.title: Mechanical Degradation and Lifetime Map
nodes.summary: A map for the time-dependent and damage-accumulating side of materials performance, including fatigue, creep, fracture toughness, and strengthening routes.

nodes.title: Mechanical Properties Map
nodes.summary: A map for the property family that controls deformation, load-bearing capacity, fracture tolerance, cyclic life, and contact durability.

nodes.title: Microscopy Diffraction and Spectroscopy Map
nodes.summary: A map for methods that resolve morphology, phase, chemistry, orientation, and bonding state across scales.

nodes.title: Processing and Structural Evolution Map
nodes.summary: A map for how thermal, chemical, and mechanical processing routes generate specific structural states through solidification, transformation, recrystallization, growth, and stress evolution.

nodes.title: Semiconductor and Thin-Film Device Map
nodes.summary: A map connecting doping, band structure, device-relevant interfaces, thin-film processing, and analysis methods for semiconductor and electronic materials.

nodes.title: Solidification Transformation and Heat-Treatment Map
nodes.summary: A map for the major thermal pathways that generate microstructure through freezing, transformation, quenching, tempering, annealing, and precipitation.

nodes.title: Structure and Length Scales Map
nodes.summary: A map for how materials structure is described from atomic arrangement through defects, interfaces, grains, phases, and hierarchical organization across length scales.

nodes.title: Structure Processing Properties Performance Map
nodes.summary: A map for the central engineering loop that links processing choices to structure, properties, and service performance.

nodes.title: Thermal and Surface Analysis Map
nodes.summary: A map for thermal transitions, decomposition, surface chemistry, and spectroscopy-driven interpretation across polymers, coatings, oxides, and electronic materials.

nodes.title: Thermal and Transport Properties Map
nodes.summary: A map for thermal response, dimensional stability, transitions, ionic transport, and coupled heat-charge transport behavior.

nodes.title: Atomic Bonding and Cohesion
nodes.summary: Atomic bonding sets the energy landscape that governs structure, stiffness, melting behavior, and many other material responses.

nodes.title: Band Structure and Electronic States
nodes.summary: Band structure and electronic states determine whether a material behaves as a metal, semiconductor, insulator, or something more complicated under applied field and temperature.

nodes.title: Contact Mechanics and Friction
nodes.summary: Contact mechanics and friction explain how surfaces transfer load, dissipate energy, and initiate wear or damage under sliding, rolling, or repeated contact.

nodes.title: Creep Mechanisms
nodes.summary: Creep mechanisms explain why materials continue deforming under sustained load, especially at elevated homologous temperature.

nodes.title: Defect Chemistry and Point Defects
nodes.summary: Defect chemistry explains how vacancies, interstitials, substitutions, and charge-compensating defects shape transport, stoichiometry, and functional response.

nodes.title: Diffusion in Solids
nodes.summary: Diffusion controls mass transport in solids and therefore underlies homogenization, sintering, precipitation, oxidation, and many heat treatments.

nodes.title: Doping and Carrier Control
nodes.summary: Doping and carrier control use compositional and defect modifications to tune carrier concentration, conductivity type, activation behavior, and interface response.

nodes.title: Electrochemical Stability
nodes.summary: Electrochemical stability describes the potential window and interfacial conditions under which a material remains chemically and electronically functional rather than decomposing.

nodes.title: Fatigue Crack Growth
nodes.summary: Fatigue crack growth explains how repeated loading gradually extends cracks even when each individual load cycle stays below immediate fracture conditions.

nodes.title: Hydrogen-Material Interactions
nodes.summary: Hydrogen-material interactions explain how hydrogen enters, diffuses through, traps within, and degrades materials through coupled chemical and mechanical pathways.

nodes.title: Nucleation and Growth
nodes.summary: Nucleation and growth govern how new phases, grains, precipitates, and structural domains first appear and then expand during transformation.

nodes.title: Phase Equilibria and Phase Diagrams
nodes.summary: Phase diagrams summarize which phases are stable under given thermodynamic conditions and composition ranges.

nodes.title: Phase Stability in Complex Alloys
nodes.summary: Phase stability in complex alloys governs whether multicomponent systems remain single-phase, decompose, order, or segregate during processing and service.

nodes.title: Phase Transformation Kinetics
nodes.summary: Transformation kinetics explain how fast a new phase appears, grows, and stabilizes during processing.

nodes.title: Polymer Viscoelasticity
nodes.summary: Polymer viscoelasticity explains why polymer deformation depends on time scale, temperature, and molecular mobility rather than behaving as purely elastic or purely viscous.

nodes.title: Recovery Recrystallization and Grain Growth
nodes.summary: Recovery, recrystallization, and grain growth describe how defect-stored energy is reduced during thermal exposure and how new grain structures form and coarsen.

nodes.title: Solidification and Microsegregation
nodes.summary: Solidification and microsegregation govern how liquid transforms into solid and how solute redistributes during freezing, often imprinting long-lived microstructural heterogeneity.

nodes.title: Strengthening Mechanisms
nodes.summary: Strengthening mechanisms raise resistance to deformation by making dislocation motion, interfacial motion, or damage initiation more difficult.

nodes.title: Thin Film Growth and Residual Stress
nodes.summary: Thin film growth and residual stress govern how films nucleate, coalesce, texture, and store internal stress during and after deposition.

nodes.title: Amorphous Structure
nodes.summary: Amorphous structure describes materials with short-range atomic order but no long-range translational periodicity, leading to distinctive transport, deformation, and thermal behavior.

nodes.title: Biocompatibility
nodes.summary: Biocompatibility describes whether a material can perform its intended biological function without producing unacceptable local or systemic response.

nodes.title: Ceramics
nodes.summary: Ceramics are often chemically stable, stiff, and temperature-resistant, but many are limited by brittleness and flaw sensitivity.

nodes.title: Coatings and Protective Layers
nodes.summary: Coatings and protective layers are engineered surface-bound systems that provide barrier, adhesion, wear, optical, electrical, or biological functions beyond the bulk substrate.

nodes.title: Composites
nodes.summary: Composites combine distinct constituents so that architecture and interfaces create a property profile no single constituent could deliver alone.

nodes.title: Crystal Defects
nodes.summary: Crystal defects include vacancies, interstitials, dislocations, and interfaces that strongly influence diffusion, plasticity, conductivity, and failure.

nodes.title: Crystal Structure
nodes.summary: Crystal structure describes the repeating atomic arrangement that sets symmetry, slip behavior, anisotropy, and many phase-dependent properties.

nodes.title: Dendritic and Cellular Solidification
nodes.summary: Dendritic and cellular solidification describe common non-planar growth morphologies that emerge when thermal and solutal fields destabilize a solidification front.

nodes.title: Dielectric Materials
nodes.summary: Dielectric materials are insulating or weakly conducting materials engineered for polarization behavior, field response, energy storage, or capacitive functionality.

nodes.title: Dislocations
nodes.summary: Dislocations are line defects that enable crystal plasticity at stresses far below the theoretical shear strength of a perfect lattice.

nodes.title: Fiber-Reinforced Composites
nodes.summary: Fiber-reinforced composites use high-aspect-ratio reinforcements and matrix-interface design to create directional stiffness, strength, and damage-tolerance profiles.

nodes.title: Grain Boundaries
nodes.summary: Grain boundaries are interfaces between differently oriented crystals that strongly influence strength, diffusion, corrosion, creep, and fracture behavior.

nodes.title: Hierarchical Structure Across Scales
nodes.summary: Hierarchical structure across scales describes how atomic, nanoscale, microscale, and mesoscale features combine to produce emergent material behavior.

nodes.title: High-Entropy Alloys
nodes.summary: High-entropy alloys are compositionally complex metallic systems designed to exploit multicomponent chemistry for unusual combinations of strength, ductility, stability, or environmental resistance.

nodes.title: Interfacial Engineering
nodes.summary: Interfacial engineering deliberately modifies surfaces and interfaces to control adhesion, transport, corrosion, reaction pathways, and mechanical load transfer.

nodes.title: Magnetic Materials
nodes.summary: Magnetic materials are materials whose electronic structure and microstructure support useful magnetization behavior for storage, sensing, motors, or shielding.

nodes.title: Material Classes
nodes.summary: Material classes group recurring families of materials by bonding, structure, processing routes, and typical property profiles.

nodes.title: Metals
nodes.summary: Metals are typically good conductors with deformable crystal structures, making them central to structural, thermal, and electrical engineering.

nodes.title: Microstructure
nodes.summary: Microstructure is the mesoscale arrangement of phases, grains, precipitates, pores, and interfaces produced by composition and processing history.

nodes.title: Nanomaterials
nodes.summary: Nanomaterials are materials whose functional or structural behavior is strongly shaped by nanoscale dimensions, interfaces, and confinement effects.

nodes.title: Particle Packing and Porosity
nodes.summary: Particle packing and porosity describe how discrete particles occupy space and how the void network they leave behind affects densification, strength, and transport.

nodes.title: Phase Fraction and Morphology
nodes.summary: Phase fraction and morphology describe how much of each phase is present and how those phases are spatially arranged, both of which strongly influence properties.

nodes.title: Planar Defects and Twins
nodes.summary: Planar defects such as stacking faults, antiphase boundaries, and twins modify local crystal registry across a plane and can strongly affect deformation, transformation, and functional response.

nodes.title: Point Defects
nodes.summary: Point defects are zero-dimensional departures from ideal atomic occupancy such as vacancies, interstitials, and substitutional atoms that strongly affect diffusion, stoichiometry, and transport.

nodes.title: Polymers
nodes.summary: Polymers are chain-based materials whose response depends strongly on molecular architecture, temperature, time scale, and processing history.

nodes.title: Precipitation Hardening
nodes.summary: Precipitation hardening strengthens a material by forming finely distributed second-phase particles that interfere with dislocation motion.

nodes.title: Residual Stress
nodes.summary: Residual stress is internal stress retained in a material after processing or service history, even when no external load is currently applied.

nodes.title: Solid Electrolytes
nodes.summary: Solid electrolytes are materials that conduct ions while constraining electrons, enabling solid-state electrochemical devices with new interface and stability challenges.

nodes.title: Surface States and Interface Traps
nodes.summary: Surface states and interface traps are electronic defect states at surfaces or interfaces that can capture carriers, alter band alignment, and degrade device behavior.

nodes.title: Texture and Anisotropy
nodes.summary: Texture and anisotropy describe how preferred orientation or directional structure causes properties and deformation behavior to depend on direction.

nodes.title: Unit Cell and Crystal Symmetry
nodes.summary: Unit cells and symmetry operations provide the compact language used to describe periodic atomic arrangement, crystallographic directions, and anisotropic behavior.

nodes.title: Carrier Mobility
nodes.summary: Carrier mobility measures how rapidly charge carriers move through a material under an applied electric field.

nodes.title: Corrosion Resistance
nodes.summary: Corrosion resistance describes how effectively a material maintains function and surface stability in a chemically or electrochemically aggressive environment.

nodes.title: Creep Resistance
nodes.summary: Creep resistance describes how effectively a material suppresses time-dependent deformation and damage under sustained load at elevated temperature.

nodes.title: Dielectric Response
nodes.summary: Dielectric response describes how a material polarizes, stores electric field energy, and leaks or breaks down under applied voltage.

nodes.title: Ductility and Plastic Flow
nodes.summary: Ductility and plastic flow describe the extent and character of irreversible deformation a material can accommodate before fracture or instability.

nodes.title: Elastic Modulus and Stiffness
nodes.summary: Elastic modulus and stiffness describe resistance to reversible deformation under load and set the baseline compliance of a material or structure.

nodes.title: Electrical Properties
nodes.summary: Electrical properties capture how carriers move, scatter, polarize, or become trapped inside a material.

nodes.title: Fatigue Resistance
nodes.summary: Fatigue resistance describes how well a material tolerates cyclic loading without crack initiation or damaging crack growth.

nodes.title: Fracture Toughness
nodes.summary: Fracture toughness measures a material's ability to resist crack extension in the presence of flaws and stress concentration.

nodes.title: Glass Transition and Thermal Transitions
nodes.summary: Glass transition and related thermal transitions describe when a material changes mobility regime, ordering state, or heat-capacity behavior with temperature.

nodes.title: Ionic Conductivity
nodes.summary: Ionic conductivity measures how easily ions move through a material under an electrochemical driving force.

nodes.title: Magnetic Response
nodes.summary: Magnetic response describes how a material magnetizes, demagnetizes, stores magnetic energy, or dissipates magnetic loss under an applied field.

nodes.title: Mechanical Properties
nodes.summary: Mechanical properties describe how a material responds to load, deformation, fracture, cyclic stress, and long-term service conditions.

nodes.title: Optical Properties
nodes.summary: Optical properties describe how a material absorbs, transmits, reflects, scatters, emits, or otherwise interacts with electromagnetic radiation in relevant wavelength ranges.

nodes.title: Oxidation Resistance
nodes.summary: Oxidation resistance describes how well a material resists high-temperature reaction with oxygen while maintaining structure and function.

nodes.title: Strength and Yield Behavior
nodes.summary: Strength and yield behavior describe when irreversible deformation begins and how much load a material can sustain before instability or failure.

nodes.title: Thermal Expansion
nodes.summary: Thermal expansion describes how a material changes dimension with temperature and is a major source of thermal mismatch stress in joined or constrained systems.

nodes.title: Thermal Properties
nodes.summary: Thermal properties describe heat storage, heat transport, expansion, and temperature stability in a material system.

nodes.title: Thermoelectric Transport Tradeoffs
nodes.summary: Thermoelectric transport tradeoffs describe the coupled and often conflicting relationships among electronic transport, thermal transport, and conversion performance in thermoelectric materials.

nodes.title: Viscoelastic Response
nodes.summary: Viscoelastic response describes time-dependent stiffness, damping, relaxation, and recovery in polymeric and other soft material systems.

nodes.title: Wear Resistance
nodes.summary: Wear resistance describes how well a material or surface maintains geometry and function under repeated contact, sliding, rolling, or abrasive interaction.

nodes.title: Additive Manufacturing
nodes.summary: Additive manufacturing builds geometry layer by layer, creating unusual thermal histories and microstructures that demand new process-structure-property reasoning.

nodes.title: Annealing
nodes.summary: Annealing uses controlled heating and cooling to reduce stored energy, alter defect structure, and drive recovery, recrystallization, grain growth, or stress relief.

nodes.title: Casting and Solidification Processing
nodes.summary: Casting and solidification processing shape components through liquid-to-solid transformation while simultaneously imposing segregation, grain structure, shrinkage, and defect evolution.

nodes.title: Doping and Activation Processing
nodes.summary: Doping and activation processing introduce and activate dopants through thermal, chemical, or growth-based routes that target carrier control without destroying structural quality.

nodes.title: Heat Treatment
nodes.summary: Heat treatment uses controlled thermal history to alter phases, defect density, and microstructure in order to shift properties.

nodes.title: Polymer Processing
nodes.summary: Polymer processing uses flow, heat, pressure, and cooling history to shape components while also controlling chain orientation, crystallinity, and residual stress.

nodes.title: Powder Processing and Compaction
nodes.summary: Powder processing and compaction create a shaped particulate body whose packing state, contact network, and green density strongly influence later densification and final structure.

nodes.title: Quenching and Tempering
nodes.summary: Quenching and tempering use rapid cooling followed by controlled reheating to create and then refine high-strength metastable microstructures.

nodes.title: Rapid Solidification
nodes.summary: Rapid solidification uses very high cooling rates to trap metastable structures, suppress segregation, and refine microstructure.

nodes.title: Sintering
nodes.summary: Sintering densifies powders through thermal activation and transport processes that reduce surface energy and pore volume.

nodes.title: Surface Engineering
nodes.summary: Surface engineering modifies near-surface composition, topology, stress state, or coatings to control wear, adhesion, corrosion, biocompatibility, and interfacial transport.

nodes.title: Surface Functionalization
nodes.summary: Surface functionalization modifies near-surface chemistry or molecular attachment to tune wetting, adhesion, bio-response, catalytic behavior, or sensing performance.

nodes.title: Thermomechanical Processing
nodes.summary: Thermomechanical processing combines deformation and thermal treatment to control texture, defect density, recrystallization, and final microstructure.

nodes.title: Thin Film Deposition
nodes.summary: Thin film deposition creates surface-bound layers whose structure and interfaces are often the main functional material rather than a minor coating.

nodes.title: Atom Probe Tomography
nodes.summary: Atom probe tomography reconstructs three-dimensional near-atomic-scale composition to reveal segregation, clustering, and nanoscale chemical gradients.

nodes.title: Atomic Force Microscopy
nodes.summary: Atomic force microscopy maps surface topography and near-surface interaction behavior at high spatial resolution through probe-sample interaction.

nodes.title: Computational Materials Design
nodes.summary: Computational materials design uses simulations, databases, and optimization workflows to narrow materials search space and explain structure-property trends.

nodes.title: Corrosion Testing
nodes.summary: Corrosion testing measures mass loss, electrochemical response, localized attack, or surface degradation under defined environmental conditions.

nodes.title: Differential Scanning Calorimetry
nodes.summary: Differential scanning calorimetry measures heat flow associated with thermal transitions such as glass transition, melting, crystallization, curing, or reaction.

nodes.title: Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy
nodes.summary: Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy probes frequency-dependent electrochemical response to separate transport, charge-transfer, and interfacial contributions.

nodes.title: Electron Backscatter Diffraction
nodes.summary: Electron backscatter diffraction maps grain orientation, phase distribution, and texture on polished surfaces inside an SEM workflow.

nodes.title: Energy-Dispersive Spectroscopy
nodes.summary: Energy-dispersive spectroscopy uses characteristic X-ray signals in electron-microscopy workflows to estimate local elemental composition.

nodes.title: Hardness Testing
nodes.summary: Hardness testing measures resistance to localized plastic deformation or indentation and is often used as a rapid proxy for strength, processing state, or surface condition.

nodes.title: Impact Testing
nodes.summary: Impact testing measures response to rapid loading and is commonly used to compare notch sensitivity and dynamic toughness-related behavior.

nodes.title: Mechanical Testing
nodes.summary: Mechanical testing translates deformation, damage, and failure behavior into measurable property data under controlled loading conditions.

nodes.title: Nanoindentation
nodes.summary: Nanoindentation measures local hardness, elastic response, and sometimes time-dependent surface or thin-film deformation behavior from instrumented indentation.

nodes.title: Raman Spectroscopy
nodes.summary: Raman spectroscopy probes vibrational signatures that reveal bonding, phase identity, disorder, stress, and some local structural changes.

nodes.title: Scanning Electron Microscopy
nodes.summary: Scanning electron microscopy reveals surface morphology, fracture features, particle shape, and microstructural contrast at useful engineering scales.

nodes.title: Tensile Testing
nodes.summary: Tensile testing measures how a material deforms and fails under controlled uniaxial tension, providing baseline strength and ductility data.

nodes.title: Thermogravimetric Analysis
nodes.summary: Thermogravimetric analysis measures mass change with temperature or time to reveal decomposition, oxidation, desorption, or reaction-driven gain and loss processes.

nodes.title: Transmission Electron Microscopy
nodes.summary: Transmission electron microscopy resolves nanoscale structure, defects, interfaces, and local crystallography beyond the reach of many bulk methods.

nodes.title: X-ray Diffraction
nodes.summary: X-ray diffraction identifies crystal structure, phase content, lattice spacing trends, and texture from diffraction patterns.

nodes.title: X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy
nodes.summary: X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy probes near-surface elemental composition and chemical states through photoelectron binding-energy analysis.

nodes.title: Brittle Fracture
nodes.summary: Brittle fracture is crack propagation with little plastic deformation, often driven by cleavage, flaw sensitivity, or weak interfaces under stress.

nodes.title: Corrosion Fatigue
nodes.summary: Corrosion fatigue is cyclic crack initiation and growth accelerated by simultaneous environmental attack and repeated loading.

nodes.title: Corrosion
nodes.summary: Corrosion is the chemically or electrochemically driven degradation of a material, often intensified by microstructural heterogeneity and environment.

nodes.title: Creep
nodes.summary: Creep is progressive time-dependent deformation and damage under sustained load, usually at elevated temperature.

nodes.title: Ductile Fracture
nodes.summary: Ductile fracture is crack growth accompanied by substantial plastic deformation, void nucleation, void growth, and void coalescence before final separation.

nodes.title: Environmental Assisted Cracking
nodes.summary: Environmental assisted cracking occurs when mechanical stress and a reactive environment combine to accelerate crack initiation or propagation beyond what either factor would cause alone.

nodes.title: Fatigue
nodes.summary: Fatigue is progressive damage and crack evolution caused by cyclic loading, often initiating from surface flaws, inclusions, or stress concentrators.

nodes.title: Fracture Mechanisms
nodes.summary: Fracture mechanisms explain how cracks initiate, propagate, and interact with microstructure under different loading and environmental conditions.

nodes.title: Hydrogen Embrittlement
nodes.summary: Hydrogen embrittlement is loss of ductility and fracture tolerance caused by hydrogen uptake and its interaction with defects, stress, and crack-tip processes.

nodes.title: Interfacial Delamination
nodes.summary: Interfacial delamination is separation along or near an interface due to weak adhesion, residual stress, cyclic loading, environmental damage, or incompatibility between joined materials.

nodes.title: Oxidation
nodes.summary: Oxidation is high-temperature chemical degradation driven by oxygen reaction, scale growth, and scale failure under thermal and environmental exposure.

nodes.title: Pitting and Localized Corrosion
nodes.summary: Pitting and localized corrosion are forms of highly non-uniform chemical attack that create concentrated damage sites capable of initiating failure disproportionately fast.

nodes.title: Stress Corrosion Cracking
nodes.summary: Stress corrosion cracking is crack initiation and growth caused by the combined action of sustained or cyclic stress and a specific aggressive environment.

nodes.title: Thermal Fatigue
nodes.summary: Thermal fatigue is damage accumulation caused by repeated temperature cycling that generates cyclic thermal stress, mismatch strain, and microstructural degradation.

nodes.title: Tribology and Wear
nodes.summary: Tribology and wear describe friction-driven surface damage, material removal, transfer, and debris-mediated degradation under contact.

nodes.title: Anode Materials
nodes.summary: Anode materials host ion insertion, alloying, plating, or conversion processes that strongly influence safety, kinetics, and cycle life.

nodes.title: Battery Materials
nodes.summary: Battery materials are engineered around coupled electrochemical, mechanical, thermal, and interfacial constraints rather than any single property.

nodes.title: Biomaterials
nodes.summary: Biomaterials are materials designed to function in biological environments where mechanics, chemistry, surface state, and host response all matter.

nodes.title: Cathode Materials
nodes.summary: Cathode materials store and release ions through redox-active host structures while balancing capacity, voltage, rate performance, and long-term stability.

nodes.title: High-Temperature Alloys
nodes.summary: High-temperature alloys are designed to retain strength, phase stability, oxidation resistance, and creep resistance under severe thermal exposure.

nodes.title: Semiconductor Materials
nodes.summary: Semiconductor materials are defined by controlled carrier behavior, defect sensitivity, and processing routes that build function from interfaces and thin layers.

nodes.title: Thermoelectric Materials
nodes.summary: Thermoelectric materials couple electrical and thermal transport so that temperature gradients can generate voltage or electrical input can drive heating and cooling.
